title: Copperplate flourishing
subtitle: Adding decorative oval loops and extension lines to pointed-pen Copperplate script
excerpt: Flourishing in Copperplate extends the ascenders and descenders of script letters into decorative loops, spirals, and sweeping lines. The strokes follow the same thick-down, thin-up rule as the script itself: every downstroke is heavy, every upstroke is a hairline. The challenge is controlling the oval shape of each loop without breaking the hairline on the return.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866). The Spencer Brothers' copybooks (1848 onwards). Both public domain. Spencerian script places flourishing at the centre of the penmanship tradition; the oval-shade discipline described here follows Spencer's own instruction. Internet Archive scans available.

glossaryTerms.definition: A decorative extension of a calligraphy letter's ascender or descender, usually an oval loop or sweeping curved line that follows the thick-thin discipline of the script.
The thin upstroke that begins many Copperplate letters, arriving at the first heavy downstroke from below and to the left.
The weight or thickness of a downstroke produced by increasing nib pressure. In Copperplate, shaded strokes are the downstrokes; unshaded strokes are hairline upstrokes.

content.text: A
flourish

in Copperplate is not a free-form decoration. Every curved element of the flourish is governed by the same oval that governs the script body: a slightly compressed circle inclined at 52 degrees from the baseline. Flourishes that depart from this oval look grafted on. The discipline is to make the flourish look inevitable, as if the letter grew naturally into the decoration.
What you need

Nib: Hunt 101 or equivalent flexible pointed nib. The nib must open smoothly under pressure and close to a hairline when pressure is released. A stiff or sprung nib makes flourishing impossible to control.
Pen holder: oblique holder with a flange that offsets the nib to the left, keeping the nib aligned with the 52-degree Copperplate slant.
Paper: heavyweight cartridge, 220 gsm. Thin paper cockles under the wet pressure strokes of a flourish.
Ink: sumi ink at a consistency thin enough to flow on a hairline stroke but not so thin it spreads. Test on a corner of the paper before beginning.
Nib angle and pen hold
Hold the pen at 54 degrees from the baseline, consistent with the Copperplate slant. The
shade
rule never changes: pressure on every downstroke, no pressure on every upstroke. The hand rests on the side of the little finger or on a tracing-paper guard to avoid smudging.
Practising the oval loop
Draw a single Copperplate oval on practice paper. Check that the inclination is 52 degrees.
Continue the upstroke past the top of the oval in a long hairline curve. Loop it back into a second oval. The second oval sits one and a half x-heights above the cap-height line.
Return the loop with a heavy downstroke, arriving back at the original oval's entry point. The result is a tall oval loop on a single consistent axis.
Practise this loop as an isolated exercise before attaching it to a letter. The loop should repeat at the same size, slant, and weight on every pass.
Applying a flourish to a Copperplate h
The ascender of a Copperplate h extends to twice the x-height above the baseline. To flourish it, write the letter normally. On completing the
entry stroke

of the ascender, continue past the cap-height line in a thin curve to the left, loop back into an oval above the ascender height, and return in a shade stroke that rejoins the standard ascender curve. The flourish extends from the ascender; it does not replace it.
Common mistakes

attrs.symptom: The loop looks pinched rather than oval
attrs.cause: The return shade stroke changed direction before completing the oval, cutting the loop short.

content.text: Plan the full arc of the oval before applying pressure. The return shade stroke should begin only when the pen is already past the widest point of the loop.

attrs.symptom: Hairline upstrokes have ink blobs at the start
attrs.cause: Too much ink on the nib when beginning an upstroke.

content.text: Begin each upstroke with the nib just touching the paper, no pooled ink at the tip. If blobs persist, the ink is too thin; stiffen with a drop of gum arabic.
